critic_raw: The baseline uses LambdaMART trained with '10-fold cross-validation on the training set' for hyperparameter selection, while the transformer uses validation set for early stopping. These are different model selection protocols. LambdaMART's cross-validation on training data may overfit the training distribution more than the transformer's validation-set early stopping. The design says 'best model then trained on all training data and evaluated on the validation set' — but this two-phase process (CV on training for selection, then validation for final check) is asymmetric with the transformer's single validation-set selection. Does this create a double advantage for LambdaMART (cross-validated hyperparameters are more robust) or a disadvantage (CV doesn't see the temporal validation distribution)?
defender_raw: Round 1: The LambdaMART hyperparameter selection uses cross-validation on training data — this is the standard approach for gradient boosting models where early stopping is not applicable. The transformer uses validation-set early stopping which is the standard for deep learning. Both protocols represent best practices for their respective model classes. For LambdaMART, cross-validation on training data gives more robust hyperparameter estimates using all training examples; the final validation evaluation is used for comparison, not for selection. For the transformer, the validation set is used for both selection and comparison. The asymmetry is a consequence of model class differences, not an experimental design flaw.

rounds.critic_followup: LambdaMART's CV is on the training data (months 1-12) which has a different temporal distribution than the validation set (months 13-15). The cross-validated hyperparameters are optimized for the training distribution but applied in the validation distribution. If there is temporal drift between months 1-12 and months 13-15, the CV-selected hyperparameters may be suboptimal for validation. Does this disadvantage LambdaMART?
rounds.defender_followup: If cross-validated hyperparameters from training are suboptimal for validation, this would manifest as lower LambdaMART validation NDCG@10 — making the LambdaMART baseline weaker, not the transformer. This is a conservative choice that makes the transformer's improvement task harder (harder to beat) or more reliable (LambdaMART isn't overfitted to the validation set). Any disadvantage to LambdaMART from temporal drift in CV would reduce LambdaMART performance, making any transformer advantage more meaningful. This is not a methodological flaw in the comparison.

adjudication_raw: One concern: asymmetric model selection protocols (CV for LambdaMART vs. validation set for transformer). Addressed: each protocol is standard for the respective model class; any disadvantage to LambdaMART makes the comparison more conservative (harder for transformer to win). Verdict: defense_wins.
